title: Three roommates and the gallon jug labeled 'antifreeze'
blurb: A toxic alcohol with a deceptive latent period. Lethal not because of the parent molecule, but because of what alcohol dehydrogenase makes from it.

patient.complaint: Vomiting, severe headache, and 'snow' in his vision — drank an unlabeled clear liquid at a party last night.

opening: 34-year-old man, present 18 hours after drinking from a clear unlabeled jug at a party. He felt 'a little drunk' last night, slept normally, woke up with severe occipital headache, nausea, and now describes seeing 'snow.' Two roommates who drank smaller amounts feel ill but well; one declines evaluation. ABG: pH 7.10, anion gap 28, lactate 4. Serum osmolar gap 22. Visual acuity is 20/200 bilaterally despite normal pupils. Funduscopic exam shows disc edema. What do you want to ask, examine, or order?
history: Healthy. No regular medications. The jug had a faded 'antifreeze' label and a sticker that said 'denatured.' He estimates he drank ~150 mL. Roommate brought the rest of the jug; it tested positive for methanol on quick screen.
exam: HR 96, BP 134/82, RR 26 with Kussmaul respirations, T 36.8, SpO2 99%. Lethargic but conversive. Pupils 4 mm and equal but visual acuity grossly impaired. Funduscopy: bilateral optic disc edema. Tachypneic, no rales. Abdomen soft.

prompts.cause: Which enzyme makes the actual toxin — and which antidote blocks it?
prompts.mechanism: Briefly: why is visual loss + Kussmaul + an osmolar AND anion gap the giveaway?

pinpoint.prompt: Click the enzyme that converts methanol into its toxic downstream metabolite (and that fomepizole blocks).
pinpoint.correctMsg: Yes — alcohol dehydrogenase. ADH happily oxidizes methanol → formaldehyde → formate (the actual neurotoxin). Fomepizole competitively inhibits ADH (Ki for methanol » Km for ethanol, so blocking ADH lets methanol be excreted unchanged via the kidney).
pinpoint.wrongMsg: Not quite. The toxic alcohols hijack the SAME enzyme that handles ethanol. Block that enzyme and the parent molecule is excreted intact.
pinpoint.revealMsg: Target: ADH (Ethanol → Acetaldehyde). For methanol AND ethylene glycol, the strategy is identical: shut down ADH (fomepizole > ethanol drip) and dialyze.

mcqs.stem: Why is formate (not methanol itself) the cause of the optic neuritis and acidosis?
choices: Formate inhibits cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) in the optic nerve and contributes a strong organic acid to the anion gap; it accumulates because human formate dehydrogenase is folate-dependent and slow
Methanol is osmotically toxic to retinal photoreceptors directly
mcqs.explanation: Formate inhibits Complex IV → ATP starvation in metabolically active tissue (retina, basal ganglia, optic nerve) → demyelination and infarction. Formate is a strong acid, contributing to the anion gap. Folate (or folinic acid) speeds formate clearance via 10-formyl-THF dehydrogenase; it is given empirically in methanol poisoning.

mcqs.stem: Why is the osmolar gap LARGE early in methanol poisoning but shrinks as the patient gets sicker?
choices: Methanol is an osmotically active small molecule; as ADH converts it to formate, the osmolar gap falls and the anion gap rises
Osmolar gap is unrelated to methanol
Lactate replaces methanol in the osmolar gap
mcqs.explanation: Methanol contributes ~3 mOsm per 10 mg/dL; as it gets metabolized to formate, methanol falls (osmolar gap shrinks) and formate rises (anion gap grows). A LARGE anion gap with a NORMAL osmolar gap is consistent with late presentation.

mcqs.stem: Why is fomepizole preferred over an ethanol drip when both are available?
choices: Predictable dosing, no CNS depression, no need for level monitoring, no hypoglycemia
Ethanol potentiates methanol toxicity at the retina
Fomepizole speeds renal excretion of methanol
mcqs.explanation: Both block ADH. Fomepizole has predictable pharmacokinetics, no CNS / respiratory depression, no continuous level monitoring, and no hypoglycemia or pancreatitis risk — easier and safer than an ethanol drip. Hemodialysis is added for high methanol levels, severe acidosis, visual impairment, or end-organ involvement.
